title: Global stock markets are too inflated and will fall, top Bank of England official warns
author: Sigma Capital
metaDescription: A top Bank of England official, Sarah Breeden, has warned that global stock markets are overvalued and are likely to fall due to mounting macroeconomic risks an
excerpt: A top Bank of England official, Sarah Breeden, has warned that global stock markets are overvalued and are likely to fall due to mounting macroeconomic risks and high valuations in AI and private credit sectors.

content.introduction: A top Bank of England official, Sarah Breeden, has warned that global stock markets are overvalued and are likely to fall due to mounting macroeconomic risks and high valuations in AI and private credit sectors.
body: The Bank of England's (BoE) deputy governor has warned of a fall in global stock markets, saying near-record valuations are unsustainable.

* Sarah Breeden, deputy governor for financial stability at the Bank of England, told the BBC that overvaluations and mounting risks were raising concerns about a looming “adjustment” in equity markets. International equity markets are priced too high and will fall, according to a senior leader at the Bank of England. Sarah Breeden, deputy governor for financial stability at the U.K.'s central bank, told the BBC in an interview published Friday that macroeconomic risks were not fully priced into equity markets. "The thing that really keeps me awake at night is the likelihood of a number of risks crystallizing at the same time — a major macroeconomic shock, confidence in private credit goes, AI and other risky valuations readjust — what happens in that environment and are we prepared for it?" Breeden said during the interview. It is also true that there have been concerns around private credit and the valuations of AI stocks," he said.
Sarah Breeden, the Bank's deputy governor, claimed current global stock market prices did not reflect the array of risks facing the global economy.

Bank of England's Deputy Governor Sarah Breeden warns that global stock markets are overvalued and may face a decline due to unpriced economic risks. She emphasizes the need for financial system resilience in case of a market adjustment.

# Global stock markets are too inflated and will fall, top Bank of England official warns

Chloe Taylor@ChloeTaylor141

WATCH LIVE

Key Points

 Sarah Breeden, deputy governor for financial stability at the Bank of England, told the BBC that overvaluations and mounting risks were raising concerns about a looming “adjustment” in equity markets.
 Global stocks have remained resilient amid the U.S.-Iran war, despite lingering uncertainty about the conflict’s trajectory.
 Major Wall Street averages hit new records this week.

In this article

 .SPX
 .IXIC

A trader works on the floor at the New York Stock Exchange (NYSE) in New York City, U.S., April 23, 2026.

Jeenah Moon | Reuters [...] Jeenah Moon | Reuters

International equity markets are priced too high and will fall, according to a senior leader at the Bank of England.

Sarah Breeden, deputy governor for financial stability at the U.K.'s central bank, told the BBC in an interview published Friday that macroeconomic risks were not fully priced into equity markets.

"There's a lot of risk out there and yet asset prices are at all-time highs," she said. "We expect there will be an adjustment at some point."

It is unusual for Bank of England officials to be so candid about expectations for capital markets. [...] Closing Bell

Iain Barnes, chief investment officer at Netwealth, told CNBC on Friday that Breeden's comments reflected a wide range of visible risks.

"Market participants are well aware of potential sources of instability, but are placing greater weight on current fundamentals — such as earnings growth and profit margins — rather than speculating on an unpredictable political direction," he said.

"The Bank needs to weigh up the drivers of inflation and growth, so asking whether a market correction would affect that is a valid point."

But Barnes noted that timing such events is difficult.

Global stock markets are too inflated and will fall, top Bank of England official warns

2

Breeden said her job was not to predict when and how much the markets fall but to ensure the financial system is ready if it does.

"What we are watching for: is how might those prices fall? Will there be a sharp adjustment downwards? And if there is such an adjustment, how will that affect the economy? I'm not saying it will happen today, tomorrow, in 12 months' time. It's ensuring that if it happens the system is resilient."

Russ Mould, investment director at AJ Bell, said it was "unusual for a Bank of England official to explicitly warn about a potential stock market pullback".

The areas of concern raised by Breeden have all been "worry points" for the market in recent months, he said, but despite these concerns "markets have wobbled and then regained their poise". [...] A pair of line charts showing the UK FTSE 100 and the US S&P 500 stock market indexes over the year to 23 April 2026. In the first chart, the FTSE 100 is shown as a red line. It has increased by 24% from around 8,400 a year ago to 10,457 on 23 April this year. The S&P 500 is shown as a blue line in the second chart and has risen by 30% from around 5,400 to 7,108. Both lines dipped in March this year but have rebounded in April.

Another area of concern is the growth in a number of funds that mimic the role of banks and lend privately to businesses. These have seen losses recently and have had to restrict the amount of money that investors can withdraw, sparking concerns of weaknesses in the financial system. [...] # Stock markets are too high and set to fall, says Bank of England deputy

Getty Images Sarah Breeden at a Bank of England meeting

The Bank of England expects stock markets around the world to fall as share prices do not reflect the many risks facing the global economy, its deputy governor has told the BBC.

Sarah Breeden said: "There's a lot of risk out there and yet asset prices are at all-time highs. We expect there will be an adjustment at some point."

It is unusual for a senior figure at the Bank to be so forthright on market movements.

Breeden, who is also the Bank's head of financial stability, declined to say when she expected markets to fall or by how much, but pointed to a number of factors that markets seemed complacent about.

# Stock markets are too high and set to fall, says Bank of England deputy

BBC
Sarah Breeden at a Bank of England meeting

The Bank of England expects stock markets around the world to fall as share prices do not reflect the many risks facing the global economy, its deputy governor has told the BBC.

Sarah Breeden said: "There's a lot of risk out there and yet asset prices are at all-time highs. We expect there will be an adjustment at some point."

It is unusual for a senior figure at the Bank to be so forthright on market movements. [...] Breeden said her job was not to predict when and how much the markets fall but to ensure the financial system is ready if it does.

"What we are watching for: is how might those prices fall? Will there be a sharp adjustment downwards? And if there is such an adjustment, how will that affect the economy? I'm not saying it will happen today, tomorrow, in 12 months' time. It's ensuring that if it happens the system is resilient."

Russ Mould, investment director at AJ Bell, said it was "unusual for a Bank of England official to explicitly warn about a potential stock market pullback".

The areas of concern raised by Breeden have all been "worry points" for the market in recent months, he said, but despite these concerns "markets have wobbled and then regained their poise". [...] It can also make it harder for businesses to raise funds, which means they might reduce or delay investment. Falling markets can also hit confidence, which might lead to companies cutting back on hiring.

The US stock market is home to the world's biggest companies and has set a series of all-time highs recently despite warnings from the International Energy Agency that the global economy is facing the biggest energy shock in history.
